transcript.transcript: I'm Tom Brown, volunteering with the Emmet Orchards Oral History Project and today is Wednesday, June 18th, 2025. I am with my good cousin John Blosser, up at the home of our first cousin, Julie Morley and her son Joe Morley, um, who inherited the house and land that belonged to our ancestors. Um, today, uh, John, I'm going to be asking about his experience with Emmet Orchards and our grandparents and perhaps some family stories he's heard. This oral history is conducted with the support of the Idaho Historical Society as part of America 250 in Idaho commemoration. So, John, thank you, uh, for joining me today and, uh, reflecting um, and as we jump in, um, John, do you give me permission to record this interview? And, uh, I do. Great. I'm going to the first thing I'm going to have you, uh, state your name and spell your last name, please. I'm John Blosser b l o s s e r. Very good. It feels so good to be on this piece of ground with you, John. Um, to start, could you tell me, uh, where you were born and about those early years of your life? I was born here in Emmett. September 10th, 1945. World War two was just over. Um, my dad was in the army at the time in the, uh, the Air Corps. And he was stationed, I believe, in, uh, in Utah when I was born. And I didn't get a chance to meet my dad for, uh, a short period of time, and I don't I don't know how long a period, but probably only a few weeks, I suppose. Um. And, of course, I don't remember any of that. I was certainly an infant, but we were in Emmett only, uh, again, a short period of time, probably, uh, I would imagine only a few months. And, um, my parents were asked to move to Spokane, Washington, to help, uh, with a small congregation there. And, uh, dad, uh, being a strong believer wanted to do that. And, uh, so we moved when I was very young to Spokane. I don't have any memory of Emmett at that time. What else was it? Early years? Yeah. And so, uh, you grew up in Spokane? I grew up in Spokane. And at least once a year, and maybe occasionally more than once a year, we would come to Emmett. And that was, uh, as I was, as I can remember, at whatever age I was, I thoroughly enjoyed coming to Emmett, uh, to see my grandparents on this property and to see my cousins. And there were Probably 3 or 4 that were about the same age, and we just enjoyed one another. And I enjoyed my aunts and uncles and I just, uh, I think about it now and even brings tears to my eyes because it just, uh, was a such a joyful thing for me to to come to Emmett and, uh, and we always normally it was in the summertime, but, uh, I, I can remember once coming on, uh, at holiday time, Christmas time and that was, that was special for me too. But it was a always, uh, always enjoyable to come to Emmett. And one of the things I remembered quite vividly, actually, that my granddad would, especially if he was having a good year in the Cherry Orchard that, uh, he'd buy me a pair of cowboy boots in town. And, uh, that was always a thrill for a little guy. And, uh, so, yeah, it was it was a it was it was great fun to be here. Be here. 4th of July. And seeing the fireworks go up over by the, the the drive in and, and sometimes there was an actual fire on the Butte and we could watch that through grandpa's. He would call them. Um, well, they were binoculars, but he'd call them. I forget exactly what he'd call them. Uh, well, I'm not remembering, but he he didn't call em binoculars. It had another name, but we we enjoyed watching whatever we could see from the roof or wherever we were out here. And the view is just phenomenal from this property over the main part of Emmett and to the Butte. It's just, uh, it's always been a thrill of mine. I we would come into town, and it was before highway 16 was where it's located today. We used to come down Freezeout Hill, and we'd always stop at the, uh, monument up there and overlook Emmett and, uh, well, that was, uh, that was always a thrill. It was always a thrill to overlook Emmett as we're coming into town. John, you have, uh, touched the chord of, uh, the emotion of of those close cousins and grandparents. I'm going to, uh, back up for just a few details. Could you please name your parents and tell us about jobs they might have had? You've mentioned your dad was involved with the congregation. And, uh, let's hear a bit about your parents names and what they did. Well, my dad was Eugene Blosser, and my mother was Hazel Bernice Brown, and she was she was born here in Emmett. At a red, red brick house just a few blocks from here. Um, which is still standing, and I believe a family member still lives there. Um, dad was was raised in, uh, Vancouver, Washington, and they were, uh, they were both affiliated with a small group of congregations, um, pretty much in the western part of the US. There was probably, maybe only, I don't know, ten, ten churches, uh, in Washington, Oregon, Idaho, and even in Arizona, where we eventually moved and, uh. Yeah. Dad. Dad. In in the in the military. In during the war, he was a, uh, instrument technician. I think he was a tech sergeant working on, uh, he was in the Air Corps. So he worked on, uh, bombers like the B-17. And he was with a wing that was a training wing for, uh, before, uh, squadrons or whatever went, went overseas. And, uh, so he he did that. And after he got out of the service and we moved to Spokane, he went to work as a, uh, he went to work in a car dealership there, working on radios and other electrical systems in in the cars. And at that time. And he did that, uh, until we, we, we left Spokane when I was 11, almost 12. And I can get into that here in a little bit if need be. But, um, so that that was dad and and he met my mom here. I believe in Emmett. They were building a building, a church building here in Emmett. And dad was a part of the building crew. Um, that was building that. And I believe that's when he met my mom. Uh. And they knew each other for I believe it was six years, uh, before they got married. And, uh, they got married in 1944, September 10th, 1944. And the reason that I know that is my birthday is September 10th, 1945. So I was born on their first wedding anniversary. John, I'm, uh, I appreciate that you brought up, uh, your dad and your folks congregation. Our congregation. Um, just noting that, um, at the Idaho State Transportation Museum is the gospel coach. Mhm. And, uh, did your father drive a car with Scripture and what was that experience like for you growing up? To answer the question. Yes, we always had Bible scripture on our cars from the as long as I can remember. And uh, it was beautifully done in Old English, uh, lettering. And, uh, it was just an important outreach for anybody that would read it. There were scriptures on there that might challenge you about your eternal, eternal life. And, uh, that was, uh, a bit of a challenge for me because, you know, I went to public schools and, and, uh, you know, I was probably the only one that had a, had a car with, I think they I forget some of the names they called it, you know, the holy holy car or, or something. The Bible car or something my some of my friends. So that was, uh, that was a bit of a challenge. Uh, but that was something that that was very important to both my parents and several other, uh, leaders within the in the group of congregations also did that with their, their cars. So, John, what do you know about how those gospel cars were paid for? Um, I think initially they were, uh, they were just personal cars. And however, you know, however, you would buy a car and and then they were painted. Now later on, there was a fund that, that the, these congregations put together for that purpose of buying gospel cars and, uh, my parents, uh, had 1 or 2 cars that way. Uh, new cars actually. And um, but but in, in the beginning they were, I believe they were mostly just personal vehicles that were, that were purchased. And is and, uh, what jobs did your father have in addition to his congregation responsibilities? Well, that's where he worked for the car dealership in Spokane. And and was a, uh, I'd say an automotive electrician in, in that sense. And, uh, yeah, he he did that and all the time that we were in Spokane. Um, and some details about your mom, um, her early life, her work. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Um. Now, Tom may know some of this better than I do, but mom was the eldest of six siblings and she, like I said, she was born here in Emmett, and, uh, they went to school here, and she actually went. She was. Well, one of the her her younger brother, Tom's dad. They actually went to school together because one of them got held back. I don't remember which one. Uh, I think mom got sick. She had scarlet fever or something, and they. They held her back. So she went all the way through high school with her brother, and, uh, and then, gosh, um, then they would have school reunions every year. Just about. clear up to maybe 60 or over 60 years that they were doing high school reunion. And that was always a a real joy for for my mom. But she there are some things I'm not sure of the exact details, but she worked. She she went to she went to college in, uh, Pocatello. Idaho State. Idaho State University. Idaho State University. She just went for a year, and it was some kind of clerical, uh, designation that she got. And, uh, then she she she came back to Emmett, and she worked in the, uh, county, the county building here in Emmett, and I don't remember what department she worked in, but she was she was there for a while, And then she again, because of her, um, relationship within the congregations, she went to California and worshiped down there and lived down there for a period of time. Now I, I don't know how long that was, but this was all, uh, prior to meeting my dad and, uh, and then, uh, as we were growing up, as I was growing up, mom was, was a homemaker, and she stayed that way. Um, uh, my, I'm, I'm the eldest of three, and, uh, she stayed that way until all the kids were through high school. And then she went to work, like, in a school library and some other things. And this was in Arizona. And, uh, So that's a little bit about her. Uh, John, you mentioned, uh, your excitement in coming to Emmett. You mentioned, uh, grandpa buying you boots, uh, when there was a good harvest. Uh, were you involved in cherry harvests or other fruit harvests here at the orchards on this piece of property? I was, but that wasn't my thing. I, I have cousins that were great cherry pickers, but I wasn't one of them. I get there's, you know, it takes a lot of cherries to fill a bucket. And, uh, I'd get pretty bored doing that. I'd probably eat as many as I picked, and that wasn't good either. But, um, no, I wasn't. Um, but part of the part, part of the reason that we were here in the summertime is that my mom would would help Uh, manage the, uh, the picking operation because they'd be transient workers that would come in and they would be given, uh, tickets with a number on them. And whenever they filled a box of cherries, they would they would put their one of their tickets on that box with their number. And, uh, then at the end of the, probably at the end of the day, uh, those boxes are throughout the day. The boxes were coming through and they were counting, they were weighing the boxes and, and then as and the workers were getting paid probably by the pound or at least by the box. I'm not sure certain. By the pound. By the pound. Yeah. And, and uh. And then they would, uh, so they would accumulate for the day and then get paid Uh, that day. And mom was involved in, in that part of it in weighing and counting and I think calculating the, uh, the wage for the for the day for each, for each worker. You talked about, uh, that your mom's job during, uh, the cherry harvest. What work do you remember Grandpa Brown doing around the orchards, or Grandma Brown doing around the orchards? Well, when I was very young, irrigation of the orchards were just, uh, just, uh, trenches, basically. And he'd we'd run the water down like a main trench, and then it would branch off into, uh, these just wood, wooden, you know, trenches in the ground. And the water would run through the orchard on, uh, in, in one section at a time, and they would often get somebody to step on the edge and it would overflow or it wouldn't, you know, there'd be some maintenance going on. And I, I was always happy to get up early in the morning and grandpa would call me and I'd, I'd get up and I was his shadow. I just followed him throughout the day, and he would go and check all the irrigation, uh, and, and reroute the water if it needed to be rerouted and, and, uh, monitor the, the cherry picking that was going on. And you had several people that were picking cherries. And then, uh, he had, I believe it was seven milking cows, and they had to be milked twice a day. And in the early going or when I was very young. He did not have any, uh, didn't have a milking machine, so they all had to be milked by hand. And, uh, that was something I couldn't could not learn. He he tried to show me, but I was never real successful at milking a cow. And, uh, then a little later, and I don't know when I maybe was ten or or whatever, he did get a milking machine, and but I think he only could milk one cow at a time with that. So he'd go from one cow to the other and they were in stalls and, uh, yeah, I can remember at least once where he also had a bull, and the bull would poke his head in the, in the door, and, and it would make the cows nervous, and he'd just squirt him with squirt, squirt the bull with milk and they run off. And so that's just one little, little memory of doing that. But he also had there were times when he had, uh, one or more pigs and certainly had chickens. He didn't take care of the chickens. Grandma took care of the chickens and, uh. Yeah. And and and and then grandma had a nice garden. In fact, we're probably sitting about where the garden was, or fairly close to a nice garden. And she would always have vegetables and, and, uh. Yeah. And, and, you know, she, she, she would, she would make breakfast in the morning. We'd be out working as much as I could work in those days. Um. But so probably by 8:00 or or so, we'd come in from, from the orchard and grandma would have breakfast. And it was always a hearty breakfast of pancakes and eggs and bacon and or ham or or whatever. And, um. That's kind of how we'd start the day, and we'd have breakfast and then go back out, uh, to whatever projects grandpa was working on, and, and, uh, come in for lunch. And lunch was always, uh, it always seemed like there were quite a few people there. I don't know who all was, but. And now. But, uh. And then grandpa would sit. He had a favorite chair. He would sit in and he'd read the mail, or he'd read the newspaper. I think the newspaper came. Came in the mailbox, and, uh, he'd he'd go through the mail and then, uh, sometime after that, we'd go back out into the, into the orchard or, or do other projects that he was, he was working on. So. John, you mentioned that, um, your parents and brothers moved from Spokane to Phoenix. Um, maybe you can mention how that came about. And then, um, uh, did Alan Brown, Hazel Brown, your grandparents, our grandparents. Did they visit you in Phoenix? They did visit us in Phoenix. And I'll tell you more. Well, they also visited us in Spokane when we were there. They would they would come. So did some of the aunts and uncles would, would come to Spokane and visit us. And that was always that was fun too. And, uh, in, uh, about 1955. Uh, well, my younger brother, the next younger brother down, Jim, had severe asthma. And, uh, the climate in Spokane was not conducive to helping him at all. And so the doctors suggested that they moved to a drier climate. And, uh, they really they they they researched a few different places. One was in, um, I believe, in Denver, and another place was in Tucson. That was the only two that I can remember. And they they decided that they would send him to this, uh, school in Tucson, a boarding school called the National Foundation for Asthmatic Children. So that was their specialty, kids with asthma. So when he was six, uh, the we, uh, I'm not exactly sure why, but we went to Portland from Spokane to Portland, and he was put on a air craft or airliner and by himself at six years old and flew to Tucson. And, uh, he was there for through this, I believe, the second grade in like kindergarten, first grade and maybe part of second grade or all of second grade. And, uh, he did he did much better there. So the parents decided that they really needed to move. And they they didn't move to Tucson only because there was not a church. One of our congregations in Tucson, the closest one was in Phoenix. So he decided to move to Phoenix. In 1957, we moved to Phoenix And, uh, but during the time Jim was there, we would go. Dad didn't get a lot of vacation. He'd get maybe one week. And maybe you stretch that on, you know, weekends on either side of it. And we would drive to Tucson from. From Spokane and, uh, to to visit Jim for only just a several days is all. And, uh, and and we would do that every the 2 or 3 summers, whatever it was. And, uh, and that was, that was a hot drive because we didn't have any air conditioning in our car. And it's the summertime in Arizona, which is pretty fierce. So and then and so then we we moved we moved to Arizona. And dad did a similar kind of work in, in Phoenix, except that working on cars he worked on, uh. Well, it could have been cars, but he worked on, uh, a company that specialized in, uh, uh, electrical, automotive parts, like starters and generators, things like that. And a lot of it was from mining equipment. So some of those, some of that equipment was so heavy, they'd have to have some kind of a mechanical lift even to, to bring it into the shop. And then he would and the owner of the company had built special, uh, testing equipment for that, for those huge pieces of, uh, of equipment that that would, uh, enable them to diagnose what the problem was and fix it. And so he did that until basically until he retired, uh, at age 62. And, uh, but then he, he also was a he was very active in the congregation and, uh, would preach some and he was kind of the number two pastor, I guess you'd say. And uh, then, uh, our pastor, uh, passed away and. His name was? His name was Joe Kelly. And he. And so dad took over being the lead, the lead minister at that time. And he did that until, I don't know, he was probably 80 years old or maybe more. Uh, um, he, he took on that responsibility. It was a small, small congregation, less than 50 people. I think 50 would have been a good turnout. out. So anyway, so he was for a while he was bi vocational, meaning he, he had a regular job and, uh, a pastoral job and that one didn't, there was no pay or anything. That was he was totally a volunteer during that. And, uh, until he until he really retired, he needed to retire. He was having some memory issues at that time and and needed to step down. So so you've got your family to Phoenix. Um, and I'm, I'm curious how, um, about you as a young man, what were some of the choices that you made? What, uh, where did you live? And and, uh, did you keep coming to Emmett as you went out on your own as a young person? Still kept coming to Emmett it because, I mean, my mom was always home. She wouldn't talk about. We're not going. We're going to Emmett. We're going home. So in her mind, Emmett was always home. And, uh, so again, probably once a year or so, we would come to Emmett. And, uh, I was able to, as I was, you know, becoming a teenager or pre-teen, I was able to spend a couple of weeks here at times and I'd help on the farm here. And, uh, you know, setting irrigation and and at that time, we had. The pipes, aluminum pipes for irrigation. And so we'd go out in the morning and do that, and I'd do that with my cousin who was basically the same age. We're like two weeks apart, so. And so we would. We would. And what was that cousin's name? That was Dave Brown. David Brown, and he was the son of Floyd Brown, who was my mother's. Second youngest. Son, I guess. Yeah. Yeah. Or brother? Brother, I mean. Yeah. Her second youngest brother. Yeah. Yeah. And, uh. So, so so I was able to come here a couple of times, uh, clear up to when I was probably 15, I suppose. Was maybe the last time I came up here and spent. Spent, uh, a couple of weeks, maybe three weeks or so here in Emmett. And, uh, what, uh, what were you doing in high school? Uh, for activities or favorite classes. And what did you do after high school? So I went to an elementary school not far from where we lived, um, called Valley View School in South Phoenix. And then I went to South Mountain High School, which was the closest high school to where we lived. And, uh, I, I could have been a pretty good student, but I didn't want to be a very good student. And so I, um, I got a car when I was 16, which I, uh, probably was not in my best interest to have a car at that age. And, and so I skipped school and my grades fell, and I finally decided that, well, I was told by a counselor that I I'd goofed off so much that I wasn't going to be able to graduate. With my class. So I decided not nuts with all this. I don't care about it anyway. So I. I decided to go in the service and uh, so I was 17 in my parents were not real thrilled with that idea, but, uh, finally they said, if that's really what you want to do, then we'll, we'll, uh, we'll okay. That we'll, we'll allow you to do it because you had to have a parent approval if you were under 18. So I went in the US Coast Guard and, and uh, in January of 63, and I went to boot camp up in the Bay Area in Alameda, California for three months. And then I went to, uh, a school electronics school in San Diego, and I was in a specialty of, uh, I guess you'd call it, uh, it's like radar for guns or for weapons. And so to help, ehm, to help ehm, our our weaponry by by radar. So I was involved in electronics in, uh, the Coast Guard was small enough, was too small to have their own schools, so they, they, uh. They sent me to a Navy school in San Diego. And kind of an interesting thing about that is speaking of the Coast Guard is that Navy School, it was a huge base. It was called the Naval Training Center, and it had, I believe, around 30,000, uh, sailors there and a few, a few coasties and, uh, but the interesting thing is, that was about the size of the entire US Coast Guard was like 30,000. So, uh, I knew more. I went there for six months. So I at the end of that time, I knew more about the Navy than I did the Coast Guard. And from there I was, uh, I was sent to, uh, uh, my orders were to Seattle to be on a Coast Guard cutter. The, uh. The, uh, Klamath, the US Coast Guard cutter. Klamath, a 255 foot cutter out of Seattle, pier 91. And from there I was, uh, we did patrols. And twice we went to Alaska for enforcing, uh, Fishing treaties that the US had with other countries like Canada, Russia, Japan and, uh, just make sure that everybody was following the agreements that they, we had with other countries. And, uh. And then the other thing that our cutter did in other cutters was, uh, this was a time when there weren't a lot of, uh, satellites. So we were we would do weather patrols out in the middle of the ocean. Uh, and what that really meant was we were we were they called a motion stations where we were in this case, we were halfway between San Francisco and Honolulu, and we'd sit out there for a few weeks, and we had good air search radar and Yeah. For weather. And so we were there to give weather information to planes flying over us, and to also be there in case of a plane going down. And they, you know, for rescue purposes. So we did that 2 or 3 times a year. We'd go out there on, on be on station. And then I went to, uh. I, I got orders to go to Hawaii. And so I, I went to Honolulu and I was aboard a very similar kind of cutter out of Hawaii, the USS, the USCG Winnebago. And from there we went. We did a, uh. The main, uh, activity there. The main assignment was to do ocean stations between Hawaii and Japan. And, uh, so it would take us, we'd spend a week to go from Honolulu to, uh, Midway Island and refuel there and then go on to the weather station, what they called Ocean Station Victor. And, uh, we would be there and then we would go on to Japan and have some, uh, uh, maintenance work done on the, on the cutter because it was less expensive to have it done over there than it was in Hawaii. So we were in there for a couple of weeks and then go back out on station and then back to back to Hawaii and uh, uh, then I so I was basically discharged at the end of that. At that time, I'd put in my, my four years at that point. So. So those years in the Coast Guard overlapped some big changes, uh, here at, uh, our grandparents orchard. What were some of the changes that you saw happen as they were aging and and there were transitions? Yeah. Yeah. Well, uh, I and I, I don't know, the years. Real well today, but but, um, even before I went into Coast Guard, uh, granddad was out, um, picking up cherry blossom or cherry boxes that had been out over the, over the winter, just a few stray boxes. And he was on a tractor and he'd, he'd see one, he'd pick it up and put it on a trailer. Well, one of them had a beehive in it, and he was bit Severely. And, uh, he ended up having a leg amputated over that. And, uh, so he couldn't he couldn't do all the things that needed to be done here on the on this little farm. And so at some point. Um, my aunt and uncle, my Aunt Doris and Uncle Dale Shaw moved on to the property here and, and, uh, started operating the, the farm, basically. And, and, uh, um, which they did continually until, uh, they're, they're obviously they're both passed now, but it's still in their family, this place. And uh, they continued to, uh, to, uh, operate fruit trees. They, they they weren't so strong on cherries. They were more. Seemed like Dale wanted to do apples were perhaps a little more profitable than cherries. And, uh, um, so things, things were changing in, in that way. And then my grandparents would come to Phoenix, um. And spend, spend some time during the winter, uh, in Phoenix at my parents home. And, uh, they so they would they would obviously they were gone from, from Emmett and uh, so they, they would, uh, in fact, they were in Emmett when I joined the Coast Guard. I mean, they were in Phoenix when I joined the Coast Guard. And, uh, a little a little thing, you know, you think about it, I. granddad wanted to. I'm not exactly sure why. I guess just show affection to me. He. He gave me a little pocket knife the day I left to go in the Coast Guard, and I. I was not able to keep it, unfortunately, that that wasn't a something that the Coast Guard, the boot camp allowed to have. And I always kind of felt bad that I, I couldn't I couldn't have kept that little pocket knife that grandpa gave me. So but it was just a small thing, and he just wanted to show a little affection that way. And so anyway, um, and then I after I got out of the service, I was, I got married in the service, and that actually didn't work out very well. And, uh, but the an interesting thing is the gal that I married in Seattle had family here in Emmett, and which was an interesting coincidence. And, and so when I went overseas to or not overseas, but when I went to Hawaii, she was not able to come with me. And so, um, she stayed with her. Stepmother here in, in Emmett during that time. So, so that was, uh, another little aspect of my life. John, uh, with that first marriage and in that time in the service, I, um, I'm remembering, um, a story that might be related to your maturing in those years, related to, uh, a loan that you wanted from your grandfather. Oh, yeah. Yeah, yeah. I, uh. I wanted to. I felt like I needed to get. I needed to get out of Dodge. And leave Emmett. Like, leave Emmett. It's like I'm done with. The? Marriage. I leave. Yeah, leave it all and head back home to Phoenix. So. But I didn't have enough money, or I thought I didn't have enough money to to do that, so I. I came over here and I talked to grandpa. Grandpa was very hard of hearing and had he'd had an accident, um, or. Well, it occurred when he worked at the sawmill here in Emmett, and he, uh, a combination of the sound of saws and a thunderstorm, uh, caused him to lose his hearing. I mean, severely lose his hearing. So anyway, it was it wasn't the easiest guy to to talk with. Um, but anyway, I decided I was going to ask him for some money, and I said, uh, grandpa, uh, I wonder if I could borrow some money from you. Well, without saying a word to me, he opens up his wallet and he hands me a $20 bill. That was the end of the conversation. I think he knew that I wanted more than that. And he was prepared to give me 20 bucks. And, uh, so that was, uh, that was kind of a subtle way of saying I'm not so sure I agree with what you're doing, John, but here's 20 bucks. So, um, as we wrap up our our time together, John, um, that's a fun story of the different generations and wisdom and, uh, Boundaries. Um, what are some reflections now that you're a grandfather? And are you a great grandfather? I am. Um, so what are some of your reflections on, um, what you might carry from all of your relationships on this little piece of property to your children, grandchildren, great grandchildren? What would you like them to remember about you that might somehow be tied to this place? Well, a few things. Both of my my older kids, my two. I have two. Two children that, uh, both of which came to Emmett and spent parts of the summer here. And so they knew. They knew the same aunts and uncles that I did and were around them. And of course, there were some different cousins at that point, but I always thought that was important, that they understood where I came from. And I was able to, uh, I don't know that I intentionally, uh, wanted to instill, uh, some of my values or whatever with them, but I think those kinds of things rubbed off and. I, you know, I've always, for me personally, coming from a family that, uh, were depression era, uh, depression era parents that were very careful about spending money didn't have a lot of money. And we're always interested in saving, uh, not only saving money, but just saving things, uh, using what they had and, and, uh, a lot of that, uh, I, I obtained myself. I'm kind of that way and, uh, um, and I think some of that, uh, my kids saw that and as affected their lives as well. So, um, I don't know if that really answers what you're asking. Um. But I'll, I'll, I'll ask, uh, a story about, uh, what you know about your grandmother, Hazel Brown, as an evangelist. And how have you carried a heritage of faith forward. Well, my faith story is is checkered, I guess you'd say. I didn't, I didn't in my early adulthood. I wasn't real interested at all in in in the values of faith as I was brought up to believe in. And, uh, Grandma Brown, uh, was a strong believer, and, uh, she, she was, uh, she was a little bit subtle in that regard. I mean, she would tell me Bible stories and, and, uh, we would, you know, we we would go to the, the weekly Sunday services and Sunday school and all that with her and, and, uh, I actually think back. Not that I really knew her. I, I, I she was around when I was pretty young. My great grandmother, um. The great grandmother being, uh, Ruth Wills. Wills? Yeah. Yeah. Now, she was she was a strong influence on the rest of the family. And, uh, where where it seemed like my that she and my Grandma Brown and my mom and her, her brothers and sisters were all, um. Uh. Converted, if you will. At around the same time in, uh, maybe the late 30s, something like that. And, uh, you always knew where Grandma Brown stood. She was. She always had a strong faith. And she would she would let you know that. And, uh, grandpa was not quite that way. And you didn't really know exactly where he stood with eternal life. And, uh. But. But you did with grandma. You did for sure. Yeah. Well, John, this has been, um, a wonderful time to sit here on, uh, the ground that's been in the family a very long time as the sun sets in. Emmett. Yeah. And I really thank you for your time and thoughts and sharing today. You're welcome. Thank you. You're welcome. For sure.

lines.line: # Introduction to the Oral History Project

> Tom Brown introduces himself as a volunteer for the Emmet Orchards Oral History Project, mentioning the date and the support from the Idaho Historical Society. He is conducting the interview with his cousin John Blosser at the home of another cousin, Julie Morley. Tom seeks John's permission to record the interview, which John grants.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Oral History Project
metadata.synopsis: Tom Brown introduces himself as a volunteer for the Emmet Orchards Oral History Project, mentioning the date and the support from the Idaho Historical Society. He is conducting the interview with his cousin John Blosser at the home of another cousin, Julie Morley. Tom seeks John's permission to record the interview, which John grants.

lines.line: # John Blosser's Early Life and Family Background

> John Blosser was born in Emmett, Idaho, shortly after World War II. His father was stationed in Utah as part of the Air Corps during his birth. John's family moved to Spokane, Washington, to assist a small congregation, marking the beginning of his childhood away from Emmett.

metadata.title: John Blosser's Early Life and Family Background
metadata.synopsis: John Blosser was born in Emmett, Idaho, shortly after World War II. His father was stationed in Utah as part of the Air Corps during his birth. John's family moved to Spokane, Washington, to assist a small congregation, marking the beginning of his childhood away from Emmett.

lines.line: # Childhood Visits to Emmett and Family Connections

> John Blosser fondly recalls his annual visits to Emmett, Idaho, where he enjoyed spending time with his grandparents and cousins on the family property. These visits were filled with joy and memorable moments, including receiving cowboy boots from his grandfather during successful cherry harvests.

metadata.title: Childhood Visits to Emmett and Family Connections
metadata.synopsis: John Blosser fondly recalls his annual visits to Emmett, Idaho, where he enjoyed spending time with his grandparents and cousins on the family property. These visits were filled with joy and memorable moments, including receiving cowboy boots from his grandfather during successful cherry harvests.

lines.line: # John's Parents' Background and Occupations

> John Blosser details his parents' backgrounds and their jobs. His father, Eugene, worked in the automotive industry, while his mother, Hazel, was involved in clerical work and later in congregational activities. Their involvement in a small group of congregations influenced much of their life and decisions, including their move to Spokane.

metadata.title: John's Parents' Background and Occupations
metadata.synopsis: John Blosser details his parents' backgrounds and their jobs. His father, Eugene, worked in the automotive industry, while his mother, Hazel, was involved in clerical work and later in congregational activities. Their involvement in a small group of congregations influenced much of their life and decisions, including their move to Spokane.

lines.line: # Experience with Gospel Cars and Religious Activities

> John discusses his family's unique practice of using gospel cars, vehicles with Bible scriptures painted on them, as a form of religious outreach. This practice was a significant part of his upbringing and was both a source of pride and a personal challenge during his school years.

metadata.title: Experience with Gospel Cars and Religious Activities
metadata.synopsis: John discusses his family's unique practice of using gospel cars, vehicles with Bible scriptures painted on them, as a form of religious outreach. This practice was a significant part of his upbringing and was both a source of pride and a personal challenge during his school years.

lines.line: # John's Personal and Family Life in Phoenix

> After moving to Phoenix due to his brother's health issues, John discusses his life changes including his schooling and decision to join the Coast Guard. His grandparents visited them in Phoenix, maintaining family connections despite the distance.

metadata.title: John's Personal and Family Life in Phoenix
metadata.synopsis: After moving to Phoenix due to his brother's health issues, John discusses his life changes including his schooling and decision to join the Coast Guard. His grandparents visited them in Phoenix, maintaining family connections despite the distance.

lines.line: # Reflections on Family Values and Legacy

> John reflects on the values passed down through his family, emphasizing thrift and the importance of family connections. He hopes these values have influenced his children and will continue to be remembered by future generations.

metadata.title: Reflections on Family Values and Legacy
metadata.synopsis: John reflects on the values passed down through his family, emphasizing thrift and the importance of family connections. He hopes these values have influenced his children and will continue to be remembered by future generations.

lines.line: # Conclusion and Expressing Gratitude

> As the interview concludes, Tom expresses his appreciation for the time spent with John, reflecting on the significance of their family history and the land that has been in their family for generations. John reciprocates the sentiment, marking a poignant end to their conversation.

metadata.title: Conclusion and Expressing Gratitude
metadata.synopsis: As the interview concludes, Tom expresses his appreciation for the time spent with John, reflecting on the significance of their family history and the land that has been in their family for generations. John reciprocates the sentiment, marking a poignant end to their conversation.

lines.line: # Introduction to the Oral History Project

> Tom Brown introduces himself as a volunteer for the Emmet Orchards Oral History Project, conducting an interview with his cousin John Blosser. The interview is part of a commemoration by the Idaho Historical Society. Tom confirms with John the permission to record the interview and outlines the purpose of their discussion, which is to explore John's experiences with Emmet Orchards and family stories.

lines.line: # John's Early Life and Family Background

> John Blosser was born in Emmett shortly after World War II, during which his father served in the Air Corps. His family moved to Spokane, Washington, to assist with a congregation, marking the beginning of John's childhood away from Emmett. He shares that his early visits to Emmett were filled with joy, particularly because of the time spent with his grandparents and cousins.

metadata.title: John's Early Life and Family Background
metadata.synopsis: John Blosser was born in Emmett shortly after World War II, during which his father served in the Air Corps. His family moved to Spokane, Washington, to assist with a congregation, marking the beginning of John's childhood away from Emmett. He shares that his early visits to Emmett were filled with joy, particularly because of the time spent with his grandparents and cousins.

lines.line: # Childhood Memories in Emmett

> John Blosser recounts his annual visits to Emmett, Idaho, where he enjoyed the local festivities and the company of his family. He vividly remembers his grandfather buying him cowboy boots during successful cherry harvests and the excitement of local events like the 4th of July fireworks.

metadata.title: Childhood Memories in Emmett
metadata.synopsis: John Blosser recounts his annual visits to Emmett, Idaho, where he enjoyed the local festivities and the company of his family. He vividly remembers his grandfather buying him cowboy boots during successful cherry harvests and the excitement of local events like the 4th of July fireworks.

lines.line: # Family Roles and Occupations

> John provides details about his parents' backgrounds and their roles within their community. His father, Eugene, worked in various technical roles before becoming a pastor, while his mother, Hazel, was involved in clerical work and later became a homemaker. Their deep involvement in their church community was a significant aspect of their lives.

metadata.title: Family Roles and Occupations
metadata.synopsis: John provides details about his parents' backgrounds and their roles within their community. His father, Eugene, worked in various technical roles before becoming a pastor, while his mother, Hazel, was involved in clerical work and later became a homemaker. Their deep involvement in their church community was a significant aspect of their lives.

lines.line: # Gospel Cars and Religious Outreach

> John discusses the use of gospel cars by his family for religious outreach, a practice that involved displaying Bible scriptures on their cars. This unique method of evangelism was a significant part of his family's religious activities, reflecting their commitment to their faith.

metadata.title: Gospel Cars and Religious Outreach
metadata.synopsis: John discusses the use of gospel cars by his family for religious outreach, a practice that involved displaying Bible scriptures on their cars. This unique method of evangelism was a significant part of his family's religious activities, reflecting their commitment to their faith.

lines.line: # John's Military Service and Personal Challenges

> John talks about his schooling and the decision to join the U.S. Coast Guard at the age of 17, influenced by his academic struggles and a desire for a new direction. His service included training in electronics and assignments on various cutters, where he participated in patrols and weather monitoring missions. He also touches on the challenges in his personal life, including a brief marriage.

metadata.title: John's Military Service and Personal Challenges

His service included training in electronics and assignments on various cutters, where he participated in patrols and weather monitoring missions. He also touches on the challenges in his personal life, including a brief marriage.

lines.line: # Reflections on Grandparents and Family Legacy

> John reflects on the influence of his grandparents, particularly their strong faith and the values they instilled in him. He discusses how these values have been passed down to his children and grandchildren, emphasizing the importance of family connections and the impact of his grandparents' legacy on his own life and beliefs.

story.title: TBrown intervew 2025 06 18 John Blosser
story.description: The interview provides an oral history documenting Tom Brown's conversation with John Blosser about their family connections to Emmett, Idaho, particularly focused on the Emmett Orchards. It delves into family dynamics, heritage, and the impact of the landscape and family legacy on Blosser's life. Topics include reminiscences of summers spent working on the orchard, the roles and careers of his parents, the challenges they faced, and the faith and values passed down through generations. The account also touches on John's personal life, including his service in the Coast Guard and the changes in his perspective over the years.
